narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model designed to forecast first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 school decile of 6 (slightly above the average of 5.71), your lower Year 3 decile of 2 (well below the average of 5.53), middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 21.30 (much lower than the typical 36.70), and your plan to attend full-time like the vast majority (over 92%) of students. It predicted a 65% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which tipped the decision to reject your application as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass and succeed long-term. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as its below-average level strongly signaled higher risk compared to students who typically pass. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing because it matched what successful students usually show. Your Year 3 decile ranking also contributed toward failure, as being in the lower part of your school's distribution there raised concerns relative to peers who passed the bar. Overall, your situation stands out from typical admitted students because while your early grades and GPA align with averages, the combination of a low LSAT and late-grade dip outweighed those positives, unlike profiles with stronger test scores and consistent performance across years. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, especially given challenges many Black male students navigate in competitive academic pipelines. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 36.70 average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your recent academic decile through targeted prep or additional coursework would also help. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, explore test-prep resources tailored to your background, and apply to programs with holistic reviews that value your unique story beyond these metrics. With focused improvements, you can reshape how models like this see your bar exam prospects.
